sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Behavioural emergencies involving acute agitation or disturbed behaviour require a structured approach prioritising de-escalation, organic cause exclusion, and — when pharmacological sedation is necessary — careful agent selection with appropriate monitoring. Sedation carries significant risk and should only be used when de-escalation has failed and a safety risk exists.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale (RASS):**

| Score | Label | Description |
|---|---|---|
| +4 | Combative | Overtly combative, violent, immediate danger to staff |
| +3 | Very Agitated | Pulls or removes tubes/lines; aggressive |
| +2 | Agitated | Frequent non-purposeful movement; fights ventilator |
| +1 | Restless | Anxious or apprehensive but movements not aggressive |
| 0 | Alert and Calm | Spontaneously pays attention to caregiver |
| −1 | Drowsy | Not fully alert; sustained awakening (>10 sec) to voice |
| −2 | Light Sedation | Briefly awakens (<10 sec) to voice; eye opening |
| −3 | Moderate Sedation | Movement or eye opening to voice — no eye contact |
| −4 | Deep Sedation | No response to voice; movement/eye opening to physical stimulus |
| −5 | Unarousable | No response to voice or physical stimulus |

**A positive RASS score alone does NOT automatically indicate sedation is required.** Sedation should only be considered when de-escalation has failed and the patient poses a safety risk.

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
**Exclude organic causes before attributing behaviour to psychiatric illness:**
- Hypoglycaemia (BGL)
- Hypoxia (SpO₂)
- Traumatic brain injury (mechanism, head assessment)
- Stroke/CVA (FAST, focal deficits)
- Medication toxicity / overdose
- Sepsis / encephalitis
- Postictal state
- Alcohol or substance withdrawal

**High-risk groups requiring modified approach:**
- Pregnant patients: Senior/STORC consultation mandatory before any sedation
- Age <16 years: Senior consultation required where practicable
- Elderly / frail: Significantly reduced doses; olanzapine preferred
- Patients with Parkinson's Disease: Olanzapine and droperidol are CONTRAINDICATED — use benzodiazepine only

**Absolutely prohibited at all times:**
- Prone positioning or face-down restraint
- Handcuffing to rear or to stretcher
- Pressure on neck, thorax, or upper abdomen
- These restraint positions risk positional asphyxia and death

**Monitoring triggers:**
- SpO₂ and EtCO₂ must be applied when consciousness drops to approximately RASS −2 or lower
- Estimated weight must be documented before medication administration
- Vital signs every 10 minutes; every 5 minutes if time-critical

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety; call for assistance if needed
- **De-escalation first — always the preferred intervention:**
  - Approach calmly with a non-threatening posture
  - Speak in a low, even tone; use the patient's name
  - Validate emotions without agreeing with delusions
  - Offer choices to restore a sense of control
  - Minimise stimulation (reduce noise, lighting, crowds)
- Vital signs including BGL and SpO₂
- Screen for suicidal ideation; remove dangerous objects from reach
- Minimum force restraint only if essential to prevent immediate harm
- Never allow handcuffing to stretcher or prone positioning

sections.title: Management – Advanced / Pharmacological Sedation
sections.content:
**Pharmacological sedation is indicated only when:**
- De-escalation has been attempted and failed
- The patient poses an ongoing safety risk to themselves or others
- A senior clinical decision has been made to proceed
- Pregnant patients: Senior/STORC consult completed

**Agent selection — step-up approach:**

**1. Olanzapine Oral Dispersible (first-line where patient can self-administer)**
- Indications: RASS +1 to +3, patient tolerates oral route
- Preferred for frail patients and dementia
- Adults <70 yr: 10 mg oral; ≥70 yr/frail: 5 mg oral
- Repeat once after 15 min if needed
- Contraindicated: Parkinson's Disease, age <6 years

**2. Droperidol IM (when oral route unavailable or ineffective)**
- Adults: 10 mg IM (5 mg in elderly/frail)
- Onset 5–15 minutes
- Contraindicated: Parkinson's Disease, known QT prolongation
- Senior consultation required

**3. Ketamine IM (when above agents are ineffective or RASS +3 to +4)**
- Adults: 4–5 mg/kg IM; titrate to effect
- Dissociative sedation — maintains airway reflexes
- Emergence reactions possible during recovery
- Senior consultation required

**4. IV Midazolam — for MAINTENANCE SEDATION ONLY (not primary agent)**
- Used after primary sedation achieved with above agents
- Adults <70 yr: 1–2 mg IV titrated (max 5 mg total)
- Adults ≥70 yr: 0.5–1 mg IV titrated (max 5 mg total)
- SpO₂ and EtCO₂ monitoring mandatory

**Post-sedation monitoring:**
- SpO₂ and EtCO₂ when RASS ≤−2
- Vital signs every 5 minutes
- Airway and respiratory effort continuously
- Never transport sedated patient supine — lateral or semi-lateral position
- Resuscitation equipment immediately available

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Sedation decisions should never be made based on requests from external agencies (e.g., police)
- Document the RASS score pre- and post-intervention, estimated weight, agent used, dose, route, time, and response
- If organic cause is identified during assessment, treat the underlying cause rather than sedating
- Excited delirium is associated with hyperthermia, rhabdomyolysis, and sudden cardiac arrest — active cooling and early escalation are priorities
- Following sedation, the patient remains the clinical responsibility of the treating clinician regardless of police or other agency involvement
